Sector Overview
India's Power Sector: Three Observations
Three empirical patterns that motivate the three research questions.
Official Data
India's Transition to Clean Energy — Ministry of Power (2025)
⌄
Non-fossil capacity: 32% (2014) → 51.93% (Dec 2025). Source: Ministry of Power, GoI.
Fossil
248,542 MW (47.4%)
Renewables incl. Hydro
266,688 MW (50.9%)
Nuclear
8,780 MW (1.7%)

Key distinction

Installed capacity ≠ electricity generated. Non-fossil capacity crossed 50% in December 2025, yet coal continues to dominate actual generation. This gap is the central puzzle this thesis investigates.

Obs 1
Coal Generation Share — Dominant and Persistent
Above 70% for most of 1991–2021 despite renewable targets
⌄
Generation mix 1991–2021. Coal (gray), renewables (pink), nuclear (green). Source: WDI.

What it shows

Coal remained above 70% for most of the sample, declining only modestly after 2018. Renewable share was negligible until the 2015 solar cost collapse drove rapid expansion. Nuclear flat at ~3% throughout.

Why coal stays dominant in generation

Coal plants run at high utilisation because PPA capacity charges are already paid — DISCOMs have an incentive to dispatch coal to recover sunk fixed costs, even when renewable variable costs are now lower.

Obs 2
Private Investment — High Volatility
USD 34bn peak (2009–10), then sharp collapse
⌄
Private investment in power sector 1991–2021. Source: WDI.

Key features

Negligible through 1990s. Rose sharply after 2003 Electricity Act. Peaked ~USD 34bn around 2009–10 coinciding with 2008 civil nuclear agreement. Collapsed and settled at lower level thereafter.

Descriptive statistics

Mean: USD 12.4bn · Std dev: USD 9.8bn · CV = 0.79 (very high volatility). Motivates Act 2: what in the energy mix explains this?

Obs 3
CO₂ Emissions — Rising Despite Cleaner Mix
241 → 1,000+ metric tonnes even as renewables expanded
⌄
Power sector CO₂ emissions 1991–2021. Source: WDI.

The paradox

Emissions rose consistently from 241 Mt in 1991 to over 1,000 Mt by 2018. Brief COVID-19 dip in 2020, then recovery. This occurred simultaneously with renewable energy expansion after 2015. Motivates Act 3.

Literature Review
Existing Research and Gaps
What existing studies have established and the four gaps this thesis fills — one per act, plus the joint VAR contribution.
Act 1
Coal and Renewable Energy
Gap: No time-series causal evidence of coal suppressing renewables
⌄
Study
What they find
Gap remaining
Shrimali et al. (2017)
DISCOM financial stress and PPA obligations are barriers to renewable financing in India
Descriptive and case-study evidence only — no formal causal test
Paija (2021)
Institutional barriers and regulatory uncertainty constrain renewable deployment
No time-series test of coal as the active binding constraint
SenGupta et al. (2026)
Energy mix and macroeconomic variables interact in a VAR system for India
Does not test whether coal's share directly crowded out renewables
This study
First Granger causal evidence: coal share predictively suppresses renewable growth
χ²=7.32, p=0.007*** · Robust across all specs · Post-2007: χ²=20.34, p<0.001
Act 2
Nuclear Energy and Private Investment
Gap: Nuclear as investment credibility signal never tested for India
⌄
Study
What they find
Gap remaining
Knutsson (2022)
Nuclear in emerging markets — cost comparisons and crowding-out of other generation
Investment signal channel not examined
North (1989)
Credible institutional commitments reduce investor uncertainty and attract capital
Theoretical framework — not applied to nuclear in India's power sector
SenGupta et al. (2026)
Macro and energy variables drive investment in India's power sector
Nuclear share not tested as a specific investment predictor
This study
Nuclear Granger-causes private investment through credibility signalling
χ²=4.46, p=0.035** · FEVD 14% at h=8 · ~4× its generation share
Act 3
CO₂ Emissions Driver
Gap: Demand vs mix decomposition not done in VAR for India
⌄
Study
What they find
Gap remaining
Pao & Tsai (2021)
EKC dynamics in India — emissions first rise then fall with income
Does not isolate the demand channel against the energy mix channel in a VAR
Debnath et al. (2022)
Energy transition and emissions in India — supply-side decarbonisation focus
Demand as primary driver not formally tested against mix variables
Copeland & Taylor (2004)
Scale vs composition effect — demand growth can outpace mix improvement in growing economies
Theoretical — not empirically applied to India's power sector
This study
No mix variable predicts CO₂. Demand coeff = 1.172***. R² jumps 0.19→0.68.
Demand is the dominant driver. Scale beats composition at India's development stage.
One integrated system — not three separate studies

All three questions estimated jointly in one five-variable VAR. The selective pattern — coal predicts renewables but not CO₂; nuclear predicts investment but not coal — only emerges because all cross-variable relationships are estimated simultaneously. No prior study tests all three in one integrated framework.

Data
Data, Variables, and Pre-Estimation Checks
Source, variable definitions, stationarity tests, cointegration tests, and lag selection — each check led to a specific modelling decision.
Variables
Data Source and Variable Definitions
World Bank WDI · India · 1991–2021 · n=31 annual
⌄
VariableDefinitionUnitMeanStd DevFeature
d_coal_shareΔ Coal % of electricity generationΔ %70.4%*3.2%*Low std dev — structurally rigid
d_renew_shareΔ Wind, solar, small hydro %Δ %3.8%*3.1%*Near-zero until 2015, then surge
d_nuclear_shareΔ Nuclear % of generationΔ %3.1%*0.5%*Flattest series — stable signal
d_private_invΔ Gross fixed capital formation, powerΔ USD12.4bn*9.8bn*Very high volatility (CV=0.79)
d_power_co2Δ CO₂ from electricity and heatΔ Mt——Consistent upward trend in levels

*Statistics in levels before differencing. VAR estimated in first differences (Δ) after confirming I(1) classification. Two additional variables used in robustness only: elec_pc (demand control) and GDP growth (omitted variable test).

Step 1
Stationarity Tests — ADF, PP, KPSS
All five variables confirmed I(1) · Decision: VAR in first differences
⌄

Why stationarity matters

If variables are non-stationary (have unit roots), regressing them in levels produces spurious results — statistically significant coefficients with no real causal content. The solution is to work in first differences, which removes the stochastic trend and makes the series stationary.

Three tests — why all three

ADF (null: unit root exists) — most common but low power in small samples. PP (null: unit root exists) — non-parametric correction for serial correlation; more robust. KPSS (null: series IS stationary) — reverses the null, providing a cross-check. All three together give a more reliable classification at n=31.

VariableADF (levels)PP (levels)KPSS (levels)ADF (Δ)PP (Δ)KPSS (Δ)Classification
d_coal_sharen.s.n.s.Rejects H₀sig***sig***n.s.I(1) ✓
d_renew_sharen.s.n.s.Rejects H₀sig***sig***n.s.I(1) ✓
d_nuclear_sharen.s.n.s.10% only (0.225)sig***sig***n.s.I(1) ✓ (2/3)
d_private_invn.s.n.s.Rejects H₀sig***sig***n.s.I(1) ✓
d_power_co2n.s.n.s.Rejects H₀sig***sig***n.s.I(1) ✓

ADF/PP null: series has unit root — n.s. = fail to reject = unit root present. KPSS null: series IS stationary — sig. = reject stationarity = unit root present. Nuclear share KPSS in levels significant at 10% only (stat=0.225, 5% cv=0.146); ADF and PP unanimously confirm I(1).

Decision: All five variables confirmed I(1)

VAR estimated entirely in first differences throughout. Regressing in levels would risk spurious regression.

Step 2
Cointegration Test — Johansen
Trace and max-eigenvalue conflict · Decision: VAR in differences adopted
⌄

What cointegration means

When I(1) variables share a stable long-run equilibrium — drifting individually but moving together — they are cointegrated. If cointegrated, a VECM is needed to capture long-run adjustment. If not, a VAR in differences is appropriate. Using the wrong model discards information.

Rank (r)Trace Stat5% CVTrace DecisionMax-Eigen Stat5% CVMax-Eigen Decision
r = 0107.2868.52Reject H₀46.2833.46Reject H₀
r ≤ 160.9947.21Reject H₀22.0227.07Fail to reject
r ≤ 238.9729.68Reject H₀15.5820.97Fail to reject
r ≤ 323.4015.41Reject H₀14.1514.07Borderline
Decision: Inconclusive — VAR in differences adopted

Three reasons: (1) n=28 after lag adjustment — Johansen trace test known to over-reject in small samples; (2) three structural breaks (2003 Electricity Act, 2008 nuclear agreement, 2015 solar shock) violate stability assumption; (3) SenGupta et al. (2026) adopt same approach on identical data. Limitation: if cointegration exists, long-run equilibrium information is discarded — results describe short-to-medium-run dynamics only.

Step 3
Lag Selection
All criteria prefer p=4 but infeasible · Decision: VAR(1) with LM validation
⌄

What lag order means

The lag order p determines how many periods of history each variable uses to predict the current period. A VAR(p) with k=5 variables requires k²p + k parameters. Too few lags: residual autocorrelation, misspecified model. Too many: exhausts degrees of freedom at n=31.

Criterionp=1p=2p=3p=4Preferred
AIC————p = 4
BIC (SBIC)————p = 4
HQIC————p = 4
FPE————p = 4
Lag orderParameters neededUsable obs. (n–p)Obs/parameter ratioFeasibility
p = 1 (adopted)5²×1 + 5 = 30301.00Minimum acceptable ✓
p = 25²×2 + 5 = 55290.53Below threshold
p = 4 (preferred)5²×4 + 5 = 105260.25Infeasible ✗
Decision: p=1 adopted · Validated by LM test

LM test at lag 1: p=0.684 · LM test at lag 2: p=0.887 · No residual autocorrelation — the VAR(1) has adequately captured the dynamic structure that p=4 would theoretically improve upon. BVAR with Minnesota priors recommended as future extension to resolve this trade-off.

Methodology
Analytical Framework: VAR(1)
A five-variable Vector Autoregression estimated in first differences. All three research questions addressed through one integrated system.

VAR(2) · Clean VAR · Post-2007 · Alt Cholesky · GDP control · Toda-Yamamoto
VAR
Why VAR? What it Produces
Joint endogeneity — three tools — one integrated system
⌄

Why VAR over regression

All five variables are jointly determined in India's power sector. Coal affects renewables through fiscal constraints, but renewable growth also affects coal dispatch. Investment affects the mix, but the mix affects investment. Simple regression imposes a causal direction — VAR does not. Each variable's equation includes lagged values of all others. The data determines what predicts what.

The VAR(1) system

𝐲ₜ = 𝐜 + 𝐀₁𝐲ₜ₋₁ + 𝐮ₜ

where 𝐲ₜ is the 5×1 vector of first-differenced variables, 𝐀₁ is the 5×5 matrix of lag coefficients, and 𝐮ₜ are reduced-form residuals.

ToolWhat it testsUsed for
Granger causalityDoes past X help predict Y beyond Y's own past?H1, H2, H3 — all three research questions
IRF (OIRF)How does Y respond to a shock in X over h periods?Magnitude and persistence of identified effects
FEVDWhat fraction of Y's forecast uncertainty is attributable to X?Quantifying nuclear's disproportionate investment influence

Cholesky ordering

The OIRF requires a Cholesky decomposition to orthogonalise contemporaneous shocks. Order: coal, renewables, nuclear, investment, CO₂ — motivated by PPA slow-moving commitments. Robustness check confirms results under alternative ordering.

Results · Act 1
The Constraint: Coal and Renewable Energy
Does coal's generation share Granger-cause suppression of renewable energy growth? Confirmed at 1% significance level.
χ² = 7.32

Year 4
Dissipates by

Granger Table
Full Granger Causality Results — Panels A and B
VAR(1) baseline · 1991–2021
⌄
Equation (Y)Excluded (X)χ²dfp-value
Panel A: Dependent variable — d_coal_share
d_coal_shared_renew_share0.07710.782
d_coal_shared_nuclear_share3.32510.068*
d_coal_shared_power_co22.07310.150
d_coal_shareALL5.01130.171
Panel B: Dependent variable — d_renew_share
d_renew_shared_coal_share7.32110.007***
d_renew_shared_nuclear_share0.75810.384
d_renew_shared_power_co20.26710.605
d_renew_shareALL8.34830.039**

***p<0.01, **p<0.05, *p<0.10. H₀: excluded variable does not Granger-cause equation variable. Bold rows correspond to H1. d_private_inv omitted from Panels A–C due to collinearity.

Interpretation

Panel B provides strong support for H1. Coal share Granger-causes renewable growth at the 1% level (χ²=7.32, p=0.007). The joint test of all excluded variables in the renewable equation is also significant at 5% (χ²=8.35, p=0.039). Critically, no other variable significantly predicts renewable growth — nuclear (p=0.384) and CO₂ (p=0.605) are both clearly insignificant. This selectivity strengthens the coal finding — it is a specific and precisely identified relationship, not a noisy system.

IRF
Impulse Response Function
Negative peak −0.45 pp at step 1 · CI entirely below zero at steps 1–2
⌄
OIRF: Renewable share response to 1 SD coal share shock. VAR(1), 1991–2021, 95% CI, 8-step horizon.

How to read

Y-axis: response in renewable share (pp). X-axis: years after shock. Solid line = point estimate. Shaded band = 95% CI. Band entirely below zero = statistically significant at that horizon.

Result

A 1 SD increase in coal share (~3.2 pp) produces a −0.45 pp response in renewable growth at step 1. CI lies entirely below zero at steps 1–2. Effect dissipates by year 4 — consistent with a fiscal constraint that binds in the short-to-medium run.

Mechanism

Coal PPA fixed capacity charges pre-commit DISCOM procurement budgets — leaving less fiscal space for renewable procurement the following year.

The Catalyst: Nuclear and Private Investment
Which component of the energy mix drives private investment? Nuclear — through a credibility signalling mechanism.

VAR(1) baseline · 1991–2021
⌄
Equation (Y)Excluded (X)χ²dfp-value
Panel C: Dependent variable — d_nuclear_share
d_nuclear_shared_coal_share0.09310.760
d_nuclear_shared_renew_share0.14210.706
d_nuclear_shared_power_co20.90310.342
d_nuclear_shareALL1.12630.771
Panel D: Dependent variable — d_private_inv
d_private_invd_coal_share0.13310.716
d_private_invd_renew_share0.09410.759
d_private_invd_nuclear_share4.45710.035**
d_private_invd_power_co20.00710.935
d_private_invALL4.87140.301

***p<0.01, **p<0.05. H₀: excluded variable does not Granger-cause equation variable. Bold row corresponds to H2.

Interpretation

Panel D shows nuclear share is the only variable that significantly predicts private investment (χ²=4.46, p=0.035). Coal (p=0.716), renewables (p=0.759), and CO₂ (p=0.935) are all clearly insignificant. The selectivity is important — nuclear alone among all energy variables has predictive content for investment. The joint test (p=0.301) is not significant at conventional levels, but this is expected when one variable drives the result and others add noise.

FEVD
Forecast Error Variance Decomposition
Nuclear accounts for ~14% at h=8 — 4× its generation share
⌄
FEVD: Nuclear share contribution to private investment forecast variance. 8-step horizon.

What FEVD shows

At each horizon h, what fraction of total forecast uncertainty about investment is attributable to nuclear shocks? By construction all five variables sum to 100%.

Result

Nuclear accounts for ~14% of investment forecast variance at h=8 — roughly four times its 2–4% generation share. This disproportionate contribution is the empirical fingerprint of the credibility signalling effect.

FEVD comparison

Own-variable (investment) accounts for ~65%. Nuclear (~14%) contributes more than coal despite coal being 18× larger in generation share. Renewables contribute minimally.

Own-variable contribution ~65% at h=8.
Nuclear ~14% at h=8.
Coal — smaller than nuclear despite 18× larger generation share.
Renewables — minimal contribution.
Results · Act 3
The Paradox: Demand Drives CO₂, Not the Mix
Is the energy mix or per-capita electricity demand the primary driver of power-sector CO₂ emissions? Answer: demand, not the mix.

***p<0.01, **p<0.05, *p<0.10. Dependent: Δpower_co2 (annual change in power sector CO₂, metric tonnes). OLS with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors. Δ = annual first difference.

Scatter
Visual Evidence: CO₂ vs Electricity Demand
Near-linear relationship confirms near-unity coefficient
⌄
Annual change in power-sector CO₂ vs annual change in per-capita electricity consumption, India 1992–2021. Fitted slope ≈ 1.17. Source: WDI.

Scale vs composition

The scale effect of rising demand growth outpaced the composition effect of a cleaner generation mix. Total absolute emissions rose even as the clean share improved. India has not achieved meaningful decoupling between electricity demand growth and emissions growth over 1991–2021.

Why coal absorbs in Model B

Coal's coefficient drops from 11.36** to insignificant 5.60 once demand is controlled. Coal and demand grew together over the sample — coal appeared to drive emissions in Model A because it was a proxy for the demand trend, not because of any direct causal relationship.

Empirical Results
Robustness Checks — All Three Acts
Six specifications verify findings are not sensitive to modelling choices. H1 and H2 survive every specification. H3 is absent everywhere.
Summary Table
Granger Causality p-values Across All Specifications
Table 4.4 from thesis
⌄
SpecificationH1: Coal→RenewH2: Nuclear→InvH3: Mix→CO₂Assessment
Spec 1: Baseline VAR(1)0.007***0.035**0.507Primary result
Spec 2: VAR(2)0.030**0.000***0.526Both H1, H2 survive; H2 strengthens dramatically
Spec 3: Clean VAR (demand control)0.002***0.032**n/aH1 strengthens; H2 survives; demand not confounding
Spec 4: Post-2007 subsample0.000***0.009**0.522Both intensify considerably post-liberalisation
Alternative Cholesky orderingRobustRobust—IRF shape preserved under reversal
GDP omitted variable control0.017**——Coal coeff −0.293** after GDP conditioning
Toda-Yamamoto augmented VARInconclusive (p=0.239)——df exhaustion severe at n=31 — result unreliable

***p<0.01, **p<0.05. H3 result is the joint test for all mix variables in the CO₂ equation. Spec 3 replaces CO₂ with elec_pc so H3 not applicable. Alt. ordering places renewables first in Cholesky decomposition.

H1
Act 1 Robustness Detail
GDP omitted variable test · Post-2007 intensification
⌄
Alternative Cholesky ordering: renewables first. Negative peak and CI below zero preserved.

GDP control

After conditioning on GDP growth, lagged coal share coefficient = −0.293, p=0.017**. GDP itself enters with coefficient = −0.107, p=0.024. The coal effect survives. GDP is not an omitted variable explaining H1.

Post-2007 intensification

χ² rises from 7.32 (full sample) to 20.34 (post-2007, p<0.001). The constraint tightened as renewable ambitions grew — when renewables became a real competitor for DISCOM budgets after the solar cost collapse, coal PPA obligations became binding.

Toda-Yamamoto note

TY F(2,13)=1.60, p=0.239. Result inconclusive due to severe degrees-of-freedom exhaustion at n=31. The TY procedure adds d_max=1 extra lags, reducing usable observations further. Not interpretable at this sample size.

H2
Act 2 Robustness Detail
VAR(2) strengthening · Alternative ordering
⌄
Alternative ordering IRF: nuclear ordered last. Result robust to Cholesky ordering assumption.

VAR(2) strengthening

In VAR(2), H2 strengthens dramatically to χ²=21.10, p<0.001. Both renewable share at L2 and nuclear share at L2 become significant predictors of investment. The strengthening at longer lags is consistent with the credibility signal operating over medium-to-long horizons.

Post-2007

In the post-2007 subsample, H2 strengthens to p=0.009**. The nuclear signalling channel appears to have become more pronounced as India's power sector deepened and private investors became more attentive to governance signals.

Conclusion · Chapter 5
Policy Implications
Three findings — three policy reframings. From renewable targets to PPA reform. From nuclear-versus-renewables to nuclear-alongside-renewables. From supply-side to demand-and-supply decarbonisation.
Policy 1
Breaking the Coal Lock-In
PPA reform is a prerequisite — not a complement — to renewable deployment
⌄

The implication

Reducing solar tariffs, raising capacity targets, or expanding green financing will not unlock renewable deployment if DISCOM procurement budgets remain pre-committed to coal PPAs. The fiscal constraint binds upstream of the renewable procurement decision. Renewable energy policy alone cannot solve a fiscal architecture problem.

Three direct interventions

1. PPA renegotiation: Renegotiation or early termination of legacy coal PPAs — particularly those signed before 2010 — would free DISCOM procurement capacity.

2. Central transition mechanism: A central payment mechanism compensating DISCOMs for stranded coal capacity costs would allow PPA restructuring without imposing the full burden on individual states.

3. Market-Based Economic Dispatch (MBED): CERC's proposed MBED would replace state-level self-scheduling with centralised merit-order dispatch, prioritising renewables (near-zero variable cost) over coal. IEEFA estimates potential cost savings of Rs.120 billion annually.

Urgency

The post-2007 intensification of crowding-out confirms the constraint is tightening as renewable ambitions grow. Every new coal PPA signed today represents a twenty-five-year obstacle to renewable procurement.

Policy 2
Nuclear Alongside Renewables
Investment climate complements — not budget competitors
⌄

The reframing

Nuclear and renewable energy are often framed as competing for the same public budget. The empirical evidence points to a complementary relationship — nuclear expansion, by signalling durable governance commitment to private investors, improves the investment climate for the broader power sector including renewables.

Post-sample corroboration

Nuclear Energy Mission (Budget 2025–26): Rs.20,000 crore allocated · 100 GW target by 2047 · Private sector participation provisions.

SHANTI Act (December 2025): Formally enables limited private participation in nuclear power generation under regulatory oversight · Statutory recognition of AERB.

Parliamentary response (February 2026): Five indigenous SMR designs for deployment by 2033 · Seventeen reactors (13,600 MW) at various stages · Roadmap to 22 GW by 2031–32.

The appropriate framing

Nuclear alongside renewables as a portfolio strategy — in which the credibility properties of nuclear investment support the private financing environment that renewable deployment depends upon.

Policy 3
Demand Management as the Missing Variable
Supply-side decarbonisation alone is insufficient
⌄

The implication

India's NDC focuses primarily on supply-side targets. The near-unity demand coefficient (1.172) implies that without demand-side intervention, each unit of per-capita consumption growth translates approximately one-for-one into CO₂ growth, regardless of mix improvements.

Specific interventions underweighted by current NDC

Appliance efficiency standards · Building energy codes · Industrial energy management requirements · Electricity pricing reform removing subsidies to high-consumption users.

The reframing

From supply-side decarbonisation to demand-and-supply decarbonisation. Supply improvements reduce emissions intensity per unit; demand management reduces the consumption volume that multiplies that intensity.

Three debates reframed by thirty years of India's own data

Coal is not simply a fuel — it is a structural lock-in in DISCOM finances. Nuclear is not simply a generation source competing with renewables — it is a governance signal shaping the investment environment. The energy mix is not, by itself, the primary emissions lever — demand management is. These reframings are corroborated by the post-sample legislative developments of 2025–2026.

Conclusion · Chapter 5
Contributions to the Literature
Three specific empirical contributions extending existing work on India's energy sector.
#ContributionPrior state of literatureWhat this thesis adds
1Coal crowding-out causal evidenceShrimali (2017) and Paija (2021) — descriptive and case-study evidence of DISCOM stress. No formal causal test.First time-series Granger evidence: coal share predictively suppresses renewable growth. p=0.007*** · Robust across all specifications.
2Nuclear investment signallingKnutsson (2022) treats nuclear as cost/capacity variable. North (1989) provides theory not applied to India.First identification of nuclear as Granger cause of private investment through credibility signalling. FEVD 14% at h=8 · ~4× generation share.
3CO₂ demand paradoxPao and Tsai (2021) and Debnath (2022) examine EKC and supply-side dynamics without isolating demand against mix in VAR.Documents empirically that demand, not the mix, drives CO₂. R² jumps 0.19→0.68 with demand coefficient 1.172***.
Conclusion · Chapter 5
Limitations
Four constraints on the findings — disclosed transparently.
#LimitationConsequence for findings
1Small sample (n=31)Preferred lag p=4 infeasible. VAR(1) adopted. Longer-lag PPA and investment cycle dynamics — economically plausible over multi-year horizons — may not be fully captured. LM diagnostics provide post-estimation support but cannot fully substitute for the preferred specification.
2Inconclusive cointegration evidenceJohansen trace and max-eigenvalue tests conflict. VAR in differences adopted. If a cointegrating relationship exists, the specification discards long-run equilibrium information contained in the levels. Results describe short-to-medium-run dynamics only — not long-run equilibrium relationships.
3Single-country designFindings are specific to India's institutional context — the DISCOM structure, PPA framework, and civil nuclear programme. Whether the coal crowding-out mechanism, nuclear signalling channel, or demand-driven emissions paradox generalise to other emerging economies with different power sector structures is an open empirical question.
4Aggregate national data (WDI)Sub-national variation in DISCOM financial health, state-level PPA portfolios, and regional renewable resource endowments — all likely important moderators of the crowding-out mechanism — cannot be captured in the aggregate annual framework.
Conclusion · Chapter 5
Directions for Future Research
Five extensions that address the current limitations and build on the findings.
#ExtensionRationaleKey reference
1Bayesian VAR with Minnesota priorsResolves the central constraint — inability to implement p=4 — by using shrinkage priors to compress parameter counts while preserving longer-lag dynamics. Would likely produce more precise estimates of both H1 and H2.Litterman (1986); SenGupta et al. (2026) apply to comparable Indian dataset
2Panel VAR across South Asian economiesTests whether the coal crowding-out and nuclear signalling mechanisms generalise regionally or are specific to India's institutional architecture. Cross-country variation in DISCOM structures and PPA frameworks exploitable empirically.—
3Sub-national analysis at state DISCOM levelThe crowding-out mechanism operates through individual DISCOM procurement decisions. State-level data from CEA annual reports would allow a more granular test of the PPA fiscal channel with substantial variation in DISCOM financial health across states.CEA state-level annual reports
4Structural VAR with institutional variablesIncludes DISCOM debt levels, aggregate PPA capacity obligations, and regulatory quality indices as explicit variables — testing the transmission mechanism directly rather than inferring it from generation share dynamics.—
5Extended sample post-2021SHANTI Act (2025), Nuclear Energy Mission (2025–26), and accelerated renewable additions of 2022–2024 represent significant post-sample developments. Extending the sample would allow the investment response to SHANTI Act provisions to be tested empirically — a direct application of the nuclear signalling channel.PIB (2025, 2026)
